county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1856 from San Francisco County. Peninsula county between San Francisco and Santa Cruz. Pre-1856 records in San Francisco (many destroyed in 1906). Significant early settlement by Irish and Italian immigrants.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Mateo County Recorder in Redwood City has deeds from 1856. Pre-1856 records were in San Francisco County — many destroyed in 1906 earthquake and fire. San Mateo County History Museum has local records. The county's development as a commuter suburb of San Francisco means many families appear in both counties' records. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check the San Mateo Times and Redwood City Tribune newspapers for obituaries. Italian immigrant farming families in the Half Moon Bay area are well documented. California State Archives may hold pre-1856 duplicates.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor-grantee deed index and deed books from 1856 to present. Continuous coverage from county formation.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1856. California statewide vital records registration began 1905; earlier records at county level.
Wills, estates, guardianships, and conservatorships from 1856.

source_profiles.source_name: San Mateo County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1860-1950. Pre-county records under parent county. 1890 census largely destroyed by fire nationally.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal census series (national 1890 loss applies)

source_profiles.coverage_notes: City directories listing residents, occupations, and addresses for Redwood City and other San Mateo County communities.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good coverage from major publishers (Polk, Pacific Telephone)

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Old Spanish Days fiesta tradition. Mission Santa Barbara (1786) — 'Queen of the Missions.' Chumash tribal heritage. Significant Mexican-era rancho grants. Oil industry from 1890s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Santa Barbara County Recorder has records from 1850. Mission Santa Barbara (1786) — the 'Queen of the Missions' — has extensive baptismal, marriage, and burial records. Check Archdiocese of Los Angeles for mission records. Santa Barbara Historical Museum has outstanding local collections. Chumash tribal records through NARA and Santa Ynez Band. Mexican-era rancho grant records are extensive (Rancho Santa Rosa, Rancho Los Alamos, etc.). FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Santa Barbara News-Press newspaper provides obituaries. Oil industry records trace families from the 1890s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Santa Barbara — index and images vary by era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptismal, marriage, and burial records from Mission Santa Barbara (1786) and Santa Ines (1804) and La Purisima Concepcion (1787). Spanish colonial era records documenting Native American converts, settlers, and soldiers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Mission records generally well preserved; some at Huntington Library or Santa Barbara Mission Archive-Library

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Mission Santa Clara de Asis (1777). San Jose was California's first civilian settlement (1777) and first state capital (1850-1851). Now Silicon Valley. Significant Italian and Portuguese farming communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Santa Clara County Recorder in San Jose has deeds from 1850. San Jose was California's first civilian settlement (1777) and first state capital (1850-1851). Mission Santa Clara de Asis (1777) records at Santa Clara University archives. History San Jose has extensive local collections. Italian and Portuguese farming families well documented in local histories. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records extensively. The San Jose Mercury News newspaper (from 1851) provides early obituaries. Mexican-era rancho grants (Rancho Rincon de San Francisquito, etc.) are essential for early families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Mission Santa Cruz (1791). Lumber industry and lime manufacturing. Chinese community in Watsonville. University of California, Santa Cruz (1965). Early resort community.
county_metadata.research_tips: Santa Cruz County Recorder has records from 1850. Mission Santa Cruz (1791) records at Archdiocese of Monterey. Santa Cruz Museum of Art and History has local collections. Lime industry records (Cowell, Holmes) trace early industrial families. Chinese community in Watsonville documented in local histories. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Santa Cruz Sentinel newspaper (from 1856) provides obituaries. UC Santa Cruz Special Collections has regional history. Felton, Boulder Creek, and Ben Lomond communities have distinctive logging heritage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Santa Cruz — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Named for Mount Shasta. Reading Springs (later Redding) was an early Gold Rush settlement. Shasta City was original county seat. Wintu tribal homeland. Major dam and water project records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Shasta County Recorder in Redding has records from 1850. Shasta State Historic Park preserves records from the original county seat. Shasta Historical Society has Gold Rush and lumber era records. Wintu tribal heritage records through NARA and tribal government. Shasta Dam (completed 1945) records at Bureau of Reclamation document the massive construction project and displaced communities. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Redding Searchlight newspaper provides obituaries. BLM land patents and mining claim records trace Gold Rush-era families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Redding — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1852 from Yuba County. Gold Rush county in the northern Sierra. Downieville was a major mining town. One of California's least populated counties. Extensive mining claim records from 1850s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sierra County Recorder in Downieville has deeds from 1852. One of California's least populated counties. Downieville was a major Gold Rush town — the Kentucky Mine Museum preserves mining heritage. Mining claim records at BLM are the primary source for tracing early families. Pre-1852 records in Yuba County. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed records. The Mountain Messenger newspaper (from 1853, California's oldest weekly) provides obituaries and community notices. Small population makes Great Register voter rolls, tax assessment records, and school census records particularly valuable.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Downieville — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1852 from Shasta and Klamath counties. Northernmost California county. Yreka was a Gold Rush town. Karuk, Shasta, and Modoc tribal homelands. Lumber industry. State of Jefferson secessionist movement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Siskiyou County Recorder in Yreka has deeds from 1852. Pre-1852 records split between Shasta and Klamath (abolished 1874) counties. Siskiyou County Museum in Yreka has extensive local history holdings. Karuk, Shasta, and Modoc tribal records through NARA and tribal governments. The State of Jefferson secessionist movement provides unusual political records. Yreka was a Gold Rush town and transportation crossroads. FamilySearch has some microfilmed county records. The Yreka Journal newspaper provides obituaries. Lumber industry records trace families working in the timber economy.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Yreka — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Named for Chief Solano of the Suisun people. Benicia was briefly state capital (1853-1854). Mare Island Naval Shipyard (1854-1996) records significant. Travis Air Force Base.
county_metadata.research_tips: Solano County Recorder in Fairfield has records from 1850. Benicia was briefly California's state capital (1853-1854); Benicia State Capitol archives preserve that era. Mare Island Naval Shipyard (1854-1996) records at NARA trace thousands of military and civilian families. Travis Air Force Base records also at NARA. Solano County Genealogical Society has indexed local records. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Vallejo Times-Herald and Fairfield Daily Republic newspapers provide obituaries. Suisun (Patwin) tribal records through NARA. The Vallejo family papers at Bancroft Library are significant.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Fairfield — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Site of the 1846 Bear Flag Revolt. Mission San Francisco Solano (1823) — northernmost California mission. Vallejo family papers significant. Wine country. Jack London's ranch in Glen Ellen.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sonoma County Recorder in Santa Rosa has records from 1850. Mission San Francisco Solano (1823) records — the last and northernmost California mission — at Archdiocese of San Francisco. The Sonoma County Library History and Genealogy collection has extensive holdings. The 1846 Bear Flag Revolt began in Sonoma Plaza. General Vallejo's Petaluma Adobe and Sonoma Barracks preserve the Mexican era. Jack London's ranch in Glen Ellen has literary and agricultural records. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Press Democrat newspaper (Santa Rosa) provides obituaries.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Santa Rosa — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1854 from Tuolumne County. Named for Estanislao, a Yokuts leader. Central Valley agriculture. Modesto founded with the railroad in 1870. Significant Assyrian, Portuguese, and Dust Bowl migrant communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Stanislaus County Recorder in Modesto has deeds from 1854. Pre-1854 records in Tuolumne County. Stanislaus County Library has local history holdings. Named for Chief Estanislao, a Yokuts leader who led a revolt against the Mexican military in 1829. Modesto was founded by the Central Pacific Railroad in 1870 — railroad records trace early families. Significant Portuguese and Assyrian immigrant communities well documented. Dust Bowl migrants settled in agricultural areas. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check the Modesto Bee newspaper for obituaries.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Modesto — index and images vary by era
